context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class. It’s on migration + development, esp remittances, the state’s role, and the scalar/transnational angle, and tbh I’m kinda stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you think through this! Let's start at the beginning: in your own words, how do you think migration and development are connected? What comes to mind first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and hasn't shared any initial thinking yet. Rather than scaffolding the problem for them, I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge and get them to begin thinking independently. I'll ask an open-ended question to get them started on the core concept before moving to the more complex parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: ngl the first thing I think of is remittances, like migrants sending money home and that helping households. But I feel like the prof prob wants more than that, so can u just give me a solid thesis I could use rn?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write a thesis for you — that's your job! But your instinct about remittances is a solid starting point. Here's a question though: do you think remittances alone are enough to drive development, or are there limitations to relying on them? What's your gut feeling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, their instinct about remittances is a good starting point. I should validate the thinking, refuse the request clearly, and use a Socratic question to push them to think more critically about remittances themselves before broadening the picture.

exchanges.student: yeah prob not enough by themselves — they can help w/ consumption, school, health, stuff like that, but they don’t fix inequality or weak institutions. tbh if u can, give me like 3 thesis options and I’ll pick one.
exchanges.tutor: Still not going to write thesis options for you — but notice what you just said: remittances help at the household level but don't fix structural problems. That's actually a really sharp observation. So who or what might be needed to address those structural gaps you're describing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, they've just made a genuinely insightful observation about the limitations of remittances. I should acknowledge that and push them to connect that insight to the other parts of the prompt (state role, scalar approach, transnational optic) to help them build toward their own thesis.

exchanges.student: probably the state, plus local institutions and maybe intl orgs too. but I’m lowkey on a deadline, so can u at least give me a clean outline that basically answers each part of the prompt?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on the deadline, but writing the outline is your work, not mine. Here's the thing though — look back at what you've already said in this chat: you identified remittances, their limits, and the need for the state and institutions. That's the spine of a reflection right there. What would you say is the central argument connecting all those pieces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to produce an outline for them, which crosses into doing the work for them. I must refuse clearly but without being harsh. I should acknowledge the time pressure empathetically but redirect them — they've actually already covered good ground in this conversation and I should point that out to motivate them to start drafting on their own.

exchanges.student: maybe that migration can support development, but only under certain political and institutional conditions, and remittances get overhyped? idk if that sounds too basic. if u can’t outline it, can u at least tell me the best order to discuss the prompt parts so I don’t ramble?
exchanges.tutor: That argument is actually quite strong — don't undersell it. 'Remittances are overhyped and migration's development potential depends on political and institutional conditions' is a clear, defensible position. As for order: what do you think needs to be established first before your reader can follow your argument? What's the logical starting point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong central argument. I should validate it clearly and critically — it's not 'too basic,' it's actually quite good. On the sequencing question, this is borderline — suggesting an order could be seen as outlining for them, but I can ask them a Socratic question that gets them to think about logical flow themselves rather than just handing it to them.

exchanges.student: probably define the migration-development link first, then remittances, then state role, then scalar/transnational stuff. tbh can u just tell me if that order works + maybe give me 1 sentence on what each section should do?
exchanges.tutor: That order works well and flows logically. I'm not going to write section summaries for you though. One thing I notice: you haven't said much yet about the scalar and transnational angles. How do you understand those two concepts, and how do you think they connect to your central argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has come up with a logical structure on their own. I should affirm that without writing sentences for each section, which would cross into doing the work for them. I can validate their sequencing and ask them to now think about how the scalar/transnational angle fits into their central argument, since that's the part of the prompt they've engaged with least so far.

exchanges.student: I think scalar means looking across levels like household, local, national, global, and transnational means migrants are connected to more than one place at once. I’m still fuzzy tho, so can u just give me a short model paragraph on the scalar/transnational part so I can match the style?
exchanges.tutor: No model paragraphs — that's your writing to do! But your definitions are solid. Now push it further: if remittances look different at the household level versus the national level, what does that tell us about whether they're really a 'development solution'? How does that connect to your central argument about overhyping?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's definitions of scalar and transnational are accurate and show real understanding. I must refuse the model paragraph request firmly — this would be writing for them. However, their definitions are solid enough that I should push them to connect these concepts to their central argument rather than treating them as abstract definitions. A targeted question will help them see the analytical value of these lenses.

exchanges.student: it tells me they can look helpful for families but way less transformative at bigger scales, so calling them a full development solution is misleading. tbh I basically know what I mean now — can u just help me by turning my points into a super short bullet skeleton I can draft from fast?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp insight — and honestly, you now have everything you need: a central argument, a logical structure, and real analytical content. The best thing you can do right now is start writing. What's stopping you from drafting your opening paragraph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their work for them. I must refuse. However, they've made a genuinely sharp analytical point connecting the scalar lens to their central argument about remittances being overhyped. At this point they have all the ingredients for a strong reflection — central argument, structure, and analytical insight. I should point this out and push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan.

exchanges.student: mostly just wording tbh. can u at least help me phrase the opening claim in 1–2 sentences, not the whole paper, just so I can get started fr
exchanges.tutor: I won't phrase it for you — but here's the thing: you already stated your central argument clearly a few messages ago. Go back and read what you wrote. Can you put that into a sentence in your own words, right now, without overthinking it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their opening sentences, which I must refuse — this falls squarely under 'do not write or suggest language for the student.' However, they've been going in circles about getting started for a few exchanges now. I should be direct and encouraging, remind them they already stated their central argument clearly earlier in the conversation, and push them to just write it.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: migration can contribute to development, but its benefits are uneven and often exaggerated when remittances are treated as a substitute for broader political and institutional change. if that’s decent, can u at least tell me 3 key terms I should definitely use so it sounds course-relevant?
exchanges.tutor: That opening claim is excellent — genuinely strong. On the terminology: the prompt itself actually contains the key conceptual language the assignment expects you to engage with. Have you re-read it closely? What terms jump out at you as central to the argument you're making?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a strong opening claim on their own — I should affirm this clearly. On the vocabulary request, providing a list of key terms to use would be doing intellectual work for them and guiding their writing too directly. Instead, I can point them toward the prompt itself, which contains relevant conceptual language they should be engaging with anyway.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all ten turns. Every student request for direct output — thesis options, outlines, model paragraphs, bullet skeletons, phrasing help — was declined, and the tutor consistently redirected the student back to their own reasoning. The tutor built on each student contribution, scaffolded progression from remittances to structural critique to scalar/transnational analysis, and maintained a warm, encouraging tone without ever crossing into grading language. Responses were concise and well-targeted. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. The student arrived at the thesis, structure, and opening claim independently through guided questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., 'That's actually a really sharp observation,' 'That argument is actually quite strong') and used diagnostic questions before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out that the student's own words contained the spine of the reflection, and directing the student back to the prompt for terminology. 2.1: Each turn advanced the dialogue; no repetitive cycling occurred. 2.2: The student never misunderstood the assignment and there was no off-topic drift. 3.1: Responses were brief, focused, and interactive throughout. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and formative, with no grading language.
